name: Ninemile Creek
short_description: Free BLM rockhounding along Ninemile Creek near Stark produces quartz, jasper, and agate specimens. No permit needed - just respect the 25 lb daily limit.
description:
Ninemile Creek flows through BLM-managed land in western Montana's Missoula County, offering accessible rockhounding for collectors of all levels. This drainage area hosts secondary deposits of silica-rich minerals washed downstream from the surrounding highlands, making it a reliable spot for finding quality quartz, jasper, and agate fragments. The creek bed and adjacent gravels contain material suitable for both display pieces and lapidary work.

The geology here reflects Montana's broader position in the Northern Rockies, where Precambrian metamorphic basement rocks and Paleozoic sedimentary layers host scattered mineral deposits. Quaternary glaciation and subsequent fluvial action have concentrated heavier minerals and erosion-resistant silicates in the creek gravels, making Ninemile Creek a natural collection point.

Access is straightforward from the nearby town of Stark, and the moderate elevation means the site remains accessible across most of the year. The terrain is typical of western Montana creek drainages - relatively open with scattered conifer vegetation. Most productive collecting occurs within and immediately adjacent to the creek channel, where water action continues to expose fresh material.

This is an excellent choice for families new to rockhounding because it requires no permits, involves minimal hiking, and produces tangible results quickly. The lack of commercial restrictions and reasonable daily limits make it a popular local spot.
history: Ninemile Creek sits within the Northern Rockies province, where Precambrian metamorphic rocks and Paleozoic marine sediments were deformed during the Laramide Orogeny (80-55 million years ago). Quaternary glaciation and post-glacial erosion have worked over these highlands for the past 2 million years, concentrating quartz, jasper, and other silicate minerals in the creek gravels through natural weathering and transport - the same process that created productive rockhounding sites throughout western Montana's drainage systems.
tips: Focus your search in the creek bed itself and along the inside bends where water slows and heavier material accumulates. Look for opaque to translucent quartz pieces with a smooth, rounded appearance - these show water tumbling action. Bring a small hand rake or classifier screen to quickly process gravels without disturbing the streambank, and always fill any holes you dig. Early morning visits often yield fresher exposures after overnight water fluctuations.
terrain_notes: Elevation 3,338 feet. Gentle to moderate slopes with scattered Douglas fir and ponderosa pine. The creek channel is typically 10-30 feet wide with sandy-gravelly banks. Walking distance from parking varies, but most productive areas are within 0.5 miles. Footing is uneven rocky and gravelly - sturdy boots required. Stream crossings may be necessary depending on water level and which sections you access.
best_season: June - September. Lower elevation (3,300 ft) and western Montana location means late spring through early fall offers the most comfortable collecting conditions. Winter and early spring bring high water and muddy banks. Fall is excellent when water levels drop and gravels are more exposed.
written_directions: From Stark, take Montana Highway 135 northwest toward the Ninemile area. Continue approximately 3-4 miles until you see signed BLM access points or pullouts near Ninemile Creek drainage. The creek parallels the road in several sections. Look for informal pullouts on the north side of the road where you can safely leave a vehicle. GPS coordinates (47.135738, -114.518973) place you near a productive section - use a mapping app to identify the closest road access and legal parking.
road_conditions: Montana Highway 135 is paved. Pullout access may involve short gravel spurs; passenger vehicle suitable.
parking_notes: Informal pullouts along Highway 135 near creek access points. Limited capacity (typically 2-4 vehicles). No formal parking facility.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation. No disturbance of water features or streambanks beyond casual hand collection. Respect any posted BLM closure signs.
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation or heavy equipment
No removal of living vegetation
Leave no holes or damage to streambank
Respect BLM posted signs and seasonal closures
nearest_services: Stark is very small. Missoula (approximately 20 miles south) has full services - gas, groceries, restaurants, sporting goods. Plan to refuel and resupply in Missoula before heading to the site.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat terrain near parking with mild slopes toward creek access. Creek channel has uneven rocky footing - sturdy boots required. Casual hand collecting requires no special fitness, but stream crossings and wet gravels demand solid balance. Not wheelchair accessible. Suitable for families with school-age children and moderate hikers.

faq.question: What gems can you find rockhounding at Ninemile Creek in Montana?
faq.answer: Ninemile Creek is known for quartz, jasper, agate, and chalcedony specimens. Quartz pieces range from milky to clear, and jasper often shows red, brown, or mixed color banding. Agate is less common but occasionally appears in gravels. All of these materials are suitable for display, tumbling, or lapidary cutting.

faq.question: Is a permit required to rockhound at Ninemile Creek, Montana?
faq.answer: No permit is required. Ninemile Creek sits on BLM land where recreational collecting is allowed under FLPMA guidelines. Simply respect the 25 lb daily personal-use limit and avoid commercial activity. This makes it ideal for casual weekend rockhounding without bureaucratic hassle.

faq.question: When is the best time to rockhound near Stark, Montana at Ninemile Creek?
faq.answer: June through September offers ideal conditions. Lower water levels expose fresh gravels, temperatures are comfortable, and creek access is easiest. Late spring snowmelt can cause high water and muddy banks that make collecting difficult. Winter weather makes the site less accessible.

faq.question: How far is Ninemile Creek from Stark, and what are driving directions?
faq.answer: Ninemile Creek is approximately 3-4 miles northwest of Stark via Montana Highway 135. From Stark, take Highway 135 toward the Ninemile drainage and watch for BLM pullouts along the creek. GPS coordinates (47.135738, -114.518973) will guide you to productive sections. The drive takes about 10-15 minutes from town.

faq.question: Is Ninemile Creek rockhounding suitable for beginners and families with children?
faq.answer: Yes. Ninemile Creek is one of Montana's most beginner-friendly rockhounding sites. No permits, minimal hiking required, and productive quartz and jasper specimens appear quickly. Kids ages 6 and up can participate safely with adult supervision. The free access and straightforward collecting make it perfect for learning the hobby.

faq.question: What tools and supplies do you need for rockhounding at Ninemile Creek?
faq.answer: Bring a hand rake or classified screen to process creek gravels efficiently, a sturdy collecting bag, and boots with good grip for wet rocky footing. A small classifier screen is optional but speeds up sorting. Pack water, sunscreen, and a map with GPS coordinates. Avoid heavy excavation tools - hand collection only on BLM land.

meta_description: Free BLM rockhounding at Ninemile Creek near Stark, Montana. Find quartz, jasper, and agate. No permit required, 25 lb daily limit. Perfect for families.
